steps.annotation: cout is the output stream. The text in quotes goes straight to the terminal.
main returns 0 to signal the program ran without errors.
cout sends the literal text between the quotes to standard output.

teachMe: cout sends the string literal hello to the terminal. No newline is added.
Two cout statements with no newline produce one continuous line: startend.
Spaces inside the quoted string are part of the output.

clozeSentence: The ___ stream prints text to the terminal.
explanation: cout is the standard output stream. It is what every Q4 print line uses.

clozeSentence: Fill in a string literal in quotes that prints the word ready.
explanation: A string literal in double quotes is the simplest thing cout can print.

clozeSentence: Every C++ statement ends with a ___.
explanation: Statements end with a semicolon. Without it, the compiler errors before the next line.

spec: Write a single line that prints the literal text greetings to the terminal using cout.
explanation: cout, then <<, then the literal in quotes, then a semicolon.

spec: Write a single cout line that prints the word ok.
explanation: cout sends the quoted string to standard output.

spec: Write a single cout line that prints the literal text done.
explanation: Use cout, the << operator, the string literal, and a semicolon.

question: Type the canonical one-line cout that prints hello.

steps.annotation: Declare and assign an int variable.
<< sends the value held by total into the cout stream.
<< feeds each value to cout left-to-right. Two arrows = two outputs in one statement.

teachMe: << takes the value of x (which is 7) and writes it as decimal digits to the terminal.
<< sends n's current value to cout. The terminal shows 100.
Two separate cout statements with no newline produce: 34. The values run together.

clozeSentence: The ___ operator sends a value into cout.
explanation: << is the stream insertion operator. It feeds the value on its right into cout on its left.

clozeSentence: Fill in the output stream that goes on the left of <<.
explanation: cout is the receiver; values flow into it through <<.

clozeSentence: Fill in the variable name whose value should print.
explanation: Putting the variable name after << prints its current value.

spec: Given int count = 5; write a single cout line that prints the value of count.
explanation: << pulls the current value out of count and writes it to the terminal.

spec: Given int total = 42; write a single cout line that prints the value of total.
explanation: Use cout << then the variable name.

spec: Given int n = 7; write a single cout line that prints the value of n.
explanation: << sends n's value to cout.

steps.annotation: Anything inside double quotes is printed verbatim, including the colon and space.
Dashes and other characters inside the quotes are printed exactly as typed.

teachMe: The literal abc inside quotes is sent to stdout.
Two literal strings concatenate on the same line: sum = value (with a trailing space before value).
Two chained string literals print in sequence on the same line.

clozeSentence: Fill in the literal that prints the word done in double quotes.
explanation: A string literal must be wrapped in double quotes.

clozeSentence: Fill in the closing double quote so the literal score= prints correctly.
explanation: Every opening quote needs a matching closing quote.

clozeSentence: Fill in the word that completes the literal status ok.
explanation: Whatever you type inside the quotes is printed exactly as is.

spec: Write a single cout line that prints the literal text result is fine.
explanation: Wrap the whole phrase in one pair of double quotes.

spec: Write a single cout line that prints the literal label score=.
explanation: The literal includes the equals sign with no space.

spec: Write a single cout line that prints the literal text loading data.
explanation: The space inside the quotes is preserved on the terminal.

steps.annotation: An integer literal goes straight to cout. No quotes; the value 12 prints as decimal digits.
Print the literal int 0.
Print the literal int 1. With no newline between, output is 01.

teachMe: An integer literal sent to cout is printed as decimal digits with no quotes around it.
cout prints the int 42 as the two decimal characters 4 and 2.
5 + 3 evaluates to 8 first, then cout prints 8.

clozeSentence: Fill in the int literal that prints 99.
explanation: Integer literals have no quotes; cout converts them to decimal text.

clozeSentence: Fill in the operator so the program prints 15.
explanation: 10 + 5 evaluates to 15 before cout prints it.

clozeSentence: Fill in the int literal that prints the digit 0.
explanation: 0 sent to cout produces a single character: 0.

spec: Write a single cout line that prints the integer literal 100.
explanation: Number literals do not need quotes when sent to cout.

spec: Write a single cout line that prints the integer literal 7.
explanation: cout prints 7 as a single decimal digit.

spec: Write a single cout line that prints the result of 4 + 3 as one expression.
explanation: cout receives the value of the expression after C++ evaluates it.

question: Type the canonical one-line cout that prints the int 42.

steps.annotation: Print row 1 then a newline. The cursor moves to the next line.
Print row 2 on the next line, then another newline.
Each endl ends the current output line and moves to a new one.

teachMe: endl prints a newline. The terminal shows hello on one line and world on the next.
endl in the middle of a chain ends the current line; what follows starts a new line.
Three lines print, each ended by endl. Each output line is independent.

clozeSentence: Fill in the manipulator that prints a newline and flushes.
explanation: endl ends the line and flushes the output buffer.

clozeSentence: Fill in the manipulator that ends the first line.
explanation: endl in a chain causes the rest of the chain to print on a new line.

clozeSentence: Fill in a literal that prints the word ready.
explanation: A literal in quotes goes before endl in the chain.

spec: Write a single cout line that prints the literal text done followed by a newline using endl.
explanation: Chain the literal then << endl to end the line.

spec: Write a single cout line that prints the literal text saved followed by endl.
explanation: endl finishes the line and flushes.

spec: Write two cout statements that print abc on one line and xyz on the next, each ended by endl.
explanation: Two statements; each uses endl to break the line.

question: Type the canonical line that prints done and ends the line.

steps.annotation: \n inside the literal is a newline character. id prints, then a line break, then name.
\n is the newline escape. Shorter than endl when a flush is not required.

teachMe: \n inside the literal moves the cursor to the next line, just like endl.
Each \n in the literal breaks to a new terminal line.
Two literals each ending in \n produce two separate lines.

clozeSentence: Fill in the escape sequence that adds a newline inside the literal.
explanation: \n inside a string literal is the newline escape.

clozeSentence: Fill in any text that prints on the next line after top.
explanation: Whatever follows the \n appears on the new line.

clozeSentence: Fill in any letter so abc prints on three lines.
explanation: The \n escapes make each character print on its own line.

spec: Write a single cout line that prints the literal text done with a newline character at the end using \n inside the quotes.
explanation: \n inside the literal is a newline. Shorter than chaining endl.

spec: Write a single cout line that prints alpha and beta on two separate lines using one literal with \n.
explanation: \n in the middle splits the literal across two terminal lines.

spec: Write a single cout line that prints a label hi followed by a newline using \n.
explanation: Use the \n escape inside the quoted literal.

steps.annotation: Each << pushes the next item onto the same output line. Three things in one statement: a label, a value, a newline.
Three values in one chain: int, literal string, int. Output: 4 + 9.

teachMe: Chained << prints label and value on the same line: x = 7.
Three pieces in one chain produce: Total: 50 pts.
Three ints chained with no separator print as 123 (no spaces).

clozeSentence: Fill in the operator that chains the value of x onto the same output statement.
explanation: Each piece in the chain is connected by <<.

clozeSentence: Fill in a string literal that labels the number.
explanation: The label is just a string literal preceding the value.

code:
int a = 1, b = 2;
cout << a << ___ << b;
clozeSentence: Fill in a single-character space literal between the two numbers.
explanation: A space literal separates the two values: 1 2.

spec: Given int x = 5; write a single cout line that prints x = 5 by chaining a label and the variable.
explanation: Two pieces chained: the label string then the variable.

spec: Given int a = 4 and int b = 5; write a single cout line that chains a, the literal +, b, and endl to produce: 4+5\n
explanation: Four pieces in one chain: int, label, int, newline.

spec: Given int n = 8; write a single cout line that prints n=8 with no spaces by chaining the label and the variable.
explanation: Chain the label literal then the variable.

steps.annotation: printf takes a format string then values. %d gets replaced by the int code.
printf with only a format string and no placeholders prints the literal text.

teachMe: printf with no placeholders prints the literal followed by a newline.
%d is replaced by the int value of n. Then \n ends the line.
Each printf call prints its format string. Two calls -> two lines.

clozeSentence: Fill in the C-style output function.
explanation: printf is the C-style formatted output function used in Q4 of SIT102.

clozeSentence: Fill in a format string that prints hello with a newline.
explanation: When there are no placeholders, printf takes only the format string.

clozeSentence: Fill in the variable that supplies the value for %d.
explanation: After the format string, you list the values that fill the % placeholders in order.

spec: Write a printf call that prints the literal text done followed by a newline with no placeholders.
explanation: printf takes just the format string when there are no values to fill in.

spec: Write a printf call that prints the literal label start followed by a newline.
explanation: Just the format string, no extra args.

spec: Write a printf call that prints the literal text loading followed by a newline.
explanation: printf with only the format string when there are no placeholders.

question: Type the canonical printf line that prints done with a trailing newline.

steps.annotation: %d in the format string is replaced by the int value of items, producing items: 12.
%d takes the int year and prints it as decimal digits.

teachMe: %d is the placeholder for an int. printf substitutes 3 in its place.
%d is replaced by score's value 88. Output: score=88.
Two %d placeholders take two int args in order. Output: 5 10.

clozeSentence: Fill in the format specifier letter for an int.
explanation: %d is the printf format specifier for a decimal int.

clozeSentence: Fill in the printf format specifier that prints an int value.
explanation: %d substitutes the matching int argument.

clozeSentence: Fill in any int literal that pairs with the second %d.
explanation: After the format string, args fill placeholders in order.

spec: Given int count = 5; write a single printf line that prints count=5 followed by a newline using %d.
explanation: %d in the format string takes the int count.

spec: Given int year = 2026; write a single printf line that prints year=2026 followed by a newline using %d.
explanation: Use %d as the placeholder; pass year as the second arg.

spec: Given int n = 9; write a single printf line that prints just the int n followed by a newline.
explanation: %d alone in the format prints the int with a trailing newline.

steps.annotation: %s expects a C-string. .c_str() converts the C++ string to a const char*.
%s is replaced by the C-string returned from status.c_str().

teachMe: %s prints the C-string. Output: Bob.
%s slot is filled by n.c_str() yielding name=Cat.
Two %s placeholders take two C-strings in order. Output: red blue.

clozeSentence: Fill in the format specifier letter for a C-string.
explanation: %s is the placeholder for a null-terminated C-string.

clozeSentence: Fill in the C++ string method that returns a C-string for printf.
explanation: .c_str() returns const char* compatible with %s.

clozeSentence: Fill in the printf format specifier for a C-string.
explanation: %s is replaced by the C-string argument.

spec: Given string name = "Joshua"; write a printf call that prints name followed by a newline using %s and .c_str().
explanation: %s requires a C-string, so use name.c_str() to convert.

spec: Given string title = "Manager"; write a printf call that prints title=Manager followed by a newline using %s.
explanation: Use %s as the placeholder and .c_str() on the C++ string.

spec: Given string code = "OK"; write a printf call that prints code=OK followed by a newline using %s.
explanation: %s plus .c_str() is the SIT102 idiom for printing strings.

question: Type the canonical printf line that prints a C++ string variable name with %s.

steps.annotation: \n in the format string is a newline character. Each printf line ends with one in SIT102 Q4.
\n at the end of the format string moves the cursor to the next line.

teachMe: Each printf ends its format string with \n, producing two separate lines.
\n at the end of the format breaks the line after the value.
Two \n in one format string produce two terminal lines.

clozeSentence: Fill in the escape sequence inside the format string that adds a newline.
explanation: \n inside the printf format is a newline character.

clozeSentence: Fill in the newline escape at the end of the format string.
explanation: Q4 print lines almost always end with \n.

clozeSentence: Fill in a format string that prints just a blank newline.
explanation: A format that is just \n prints a single empty line.

spec: Write a printf call whose format string is just a newline so it prints a blank line.
explanation: A bare \n format prints an empty line.

spec: Write a printf call that prints two lines hi and bye in one call using \n inside the format string.
explanation: Two \n make two lines from one format string.

spec: Write a printf call that prints the literal text ok with a trailing newline.
explanation: End the format with \n to break to a new line.

steps.annotation: First arg is the format string. The id value goes in second to fill %d.
Format string always comes first; args follow in placeholder order.

teachMe: First arg is the format. n is the second arg, filling %d.
Format string is first; a fills the first %d, b fills the second.
When no placeholders exist, the format string itself is the entire output.

clozeSentence: Fill in the first arg: a format string that prints x=3 with a newline.
explanation: First arg of printf must be the format string.

clozeSentence: Fill in the second arg supplying the int for %d.
explanation: Format goes first, then values in placeholder order.

clozeSentence: Fill in the second arg supplying the C-string for %s.
explanation: %s requires a C-string; use .c_str() on a C++ string.

spec: Given int score = 90; write a printf call where the first arg is the format string score=%d\n and the second arg is score.
explanation: Format first, then the value.

spec: Given int x = 4 and int y = 7; write a printf call with format x=%d y=%d\n and args x then y.
explanation: Format string first; args in placeholder order.

spec: Given int v = 2; write a printf call whose first arg is the format "v=%d\n" and whose second arg is v.
explanation: Format always first; %d gets v.

steps.annotation: Three %d placeholders match three int args in order: 2, 3, 5.
%s gets the C-string from n.c_str(); %d gets the int age. Order matters.

teachMe: Three %d placeholders are filled by 2, 3, 5 in order.
a, b, c fill the three %d placeholders in order: 10 20 30.
%s pulls the first arg, %d the second. Output: Eve: 100.

clozeSentence: Fill in the second int argument to match the second %d placeholder.
explanation: Args after the format fill placeholders left-to-right.

clozeSentence: Fill in the second arg matching the %d placeholder.
explanation: %s takes n.c_str(); %d takes age.

clozeSentence: Fill in any int literal that matches the third %d placeholder.
explanation: Three %d slots take three int args in order.

spec: Given int x = 4 and int y = 5; write a printf call with format x=%d y=%d\n that fills both placeholders with x then y.
explanation: Two %d placeholders take two int args in order.

spec: Given string name = "Eve" and int age = 22; write a printf call that prints Eve is 22\n using %s and %d in that order.
explanation: %s pulls the C-string first, then %d takes the int.

spec: Given int a = 1, int b = 2, int c = 3; write a printf call that prints 1-2-3\n using three %d placeholders.
explanation: Three placeholders, three args, in order.

question: Type the canonical printf line that prints int x followed by string s using %d and %s in order.

Write one cout line that prints ready then endl.

Given int v write a one-line printf that prints v=V followed by \n using %d.

prompt: Given string s write a one-line printf that prints s with %s and a trailing newline.

Given string title write a one-line printf with %s and \n.

Write the same pattern for the literal gamma followed by endl.

Example B: printf done with newline

Write the same pattern: declare int c = 3; print it with cout.
